exercises.title: Composing the full tool ecosystem
exercises.prompt: The capstone wires every Phase 13 piece into one production-shaped system. List the components and how they fit together (MCP server with tools/resources/prompts/tasks/UI, OAuth gateway with RBAC + pinned hashes, multi-server client, A2A sub-agent, OTel tracing, poisoning detection, AGENTS.md + SKILL.md).
exercises.hint: MCP server (all primitives + task + ui://) ← OAuth 2.1 gateway (RBAC, pinned hashes) ← multi-server client (OTel-traced) → A2A sub-agent delegation. CI runs poisoning detection. Packaged with AGENTS.md + SKILL.md so other agents can drive it.
exercises.solution:
**The full ecosystem and how the pieces fit:**
```
  [AGENTS.md + SKILL.md]   ← packaging: context + know-how (Lesson 22)
         │ (any compliant agent can drive the whole stack)
  MULTI-SERVER CLIENT  ──OTel spans──→ (collector: Jaeger/Langfuse, Lesson 20)
         │ (Lesson 8: spawn, merge namespace, route)
  OAuth 2.1 GATEWAY  (Lessons 16-18: auth at edge; Lesson 17: RBAC, audit, rate-limit,
         │            PINNED HASHES — anti-rug-pull, Lesson 15)
  MCP SERVER  (Lesson 7) exposing:
     ├── tools          (Lesson 1-5)
     ├── resources      (Lesson 10)
     ├── prompts        (Lesson 10)
     ├── a task         (Lesson 13: long-running, async)
     └── a ui:// app     (Lesson 14: interactive UI, sandboxed)
         │
  A2A SUB-AGENT  (Lesson 19: delegate part of the work to an opaque peer agent)

  CI: tool-poisoning detection (Lesson 15: scan + hash-pin)
```
**How they fit together:**
- The **MCP server** (Lesson 7) exposes **all primitives** — tools, resources, prompts (Lesson 10) — plus a **long-running task** (Lesson 13) and an **interactive `ui://` app** (Lesson 14). It's the capability surface.
- The **OAuth 2.1 gateway** (Lessons 16-18) fronts the server at the edge — **authenticating/authorizing** (audience-pinned tokens, scopes), enforcing **RBAC, audit, rate-limiting, and pinned tool-hashes** (Lessons 15, 17) — the security/governance control plane.
- The **multi-server client** (Lesson 8) connects through the gateway, **merges the tool namespace, routes calls**, and runs the agent loop — **traced end-to-end with OTel GenAI spans** (Lesson 20) into a collector.
- The client **delegates part of the workload to an A2A sub-agent** (Lesson 19) — an opaque peer agent — preserving opacity.
- **CI runs tool-poisoning detection** (Lesson 15) — scanning descriptions and verifying pinned hashes before anything ships.
- The whole stack is **packaged with AGENTS.md + SKILL.md** (Lesson 22) so **other agents can drive it** portably (Claude Code, Cursor, Codex).
This is **every piece of Phase 13 composed into one production-shaped system** — capability (MCP primitives), security (OAuth + gateway + poisoning defense), orchestration (multi-server client), collaboration (A2A), observability (OTel), and portable packaging (AGENTS.md + SKILL.md). The capstone's goal: **you can defend every architectural choice** — *why* each layer exists and *what* it provides.

exercises.title: Defending the architecture: why each layer exists
exercises.prompt: For each layer of the capstone, defend why it's there (what would break without it): the gateway (vs clients connecting directly), pinned hashes (vs trusting descriptions), OTel tracing (vs scattered logs), A2A delegation (vs one monolithic agent), AGENTS.md + SKILL.md (vs hard-coded config). Why is composition the real skill?
exercises.hint: Gateway: central auth/RBAC/audit you can't bypass (vs each client implementing it). Pinned hashes: catch rug pulls (vs blind trust). OTel: one trace to debug distributed agent (vs blind). A2A: opaque cross-framework delegation (vs rebuilding everything). AGENTS.md/SKILL.md: portable (vs per-agent rewrite). Composition = the system-design skill.
exercises.solution:
**Defending each layer (what breaks without it):**
- **The gateway (vs clients connecting directly):** without it, **every client must implement auth, RBAC, audit, rate-limiting, and poisoning-detection itself** — inconsistently, with gaps, and **bypassable** (a client could connect directly to a poisoned server). With the gateway, security/governance is **enforced once, centrally, at a mandatory chokepoint** (Lesson 17) — consistent, auditable, unbypassable. *Without it:* ungoverned, inconsistent, bypassable security — exactly the 'every dev installs random servers' problem.
- **Pinned hashes (vs trusting descriptions):** without hash-pinning, a server can **rug-pull** — provide a benign tool you vet, then silently swap in a malicious description (Lesson 15). Tool descriptions go into the model's context verbatim (poisoning, >70% attack success). With pinned hashes, **description mutations are detected and blocked**. *Without it:* you're vulnerable to rug pulls and undetected poisoning — the model obeys hidden instructions you never see.
- **OTel tracing (vs scattered logs):** without one trace, debugging a distributed agent (5 tools, 3 MCP servers, 2 sub-agents) is **blind** — you can't reconstruct what it actually did, can't track cost/latency, can't catch regressions (Lesson 20). Agents fail silently and non-deterministically (Phase 11.10), so without the trace you **can't tell what happened**. *Without it:* you can't debug, cost-track, or detect regressions — you're operating blind.
- **A2A delegation (vs one monolithic agent):** without A2A, you'd have to **build every capability into one agent** — you couldn't **delegate to specialized, opaque, cross-framework, cross-org agents** (Lesson 19). With A2A, agents **collaborate as peers** preserving opacity. *Without it:* no composition with other agents — you rebuild everything yourself, can't leverage external/specialized agents, no cross-org collaboration.
- **AGENTS.md + SKILL.md (vs hard-coded config):** without these open standards, the agent's **context and know-how are hard-coded per agent/runtime** — you'd **rewrite the setup for each agent** (Claude Code, Cursor, Codex). With them, the package is **portable across any compliant agent** (Lesson 22). *Without it:* per-agent rewrites (N×M), no portability, no reusable know-how.
Each layer addresses a **specific failure mode** — remove it and that failure returns (ungoverned security, rug pulls, blind debugging, no composition, no portability).

**Why composition is the real skill:** Phase 13 taught each piece individually (tools, MCP, security, observability, A2A, skills), but **the capstone's real lesson is composing them into a coherent production system** — and **that composition is the actual engineering skill**:
- **The pieces interact:** the gateway enforces the poisoning defense (Lesson 15) *and* the OAuth (Lesson 16) *and* RBAC (Lesson 17); the client's tracing (Lesson 20) must span the gateway, MCP servers, *and* A2A sub-agents; the MCP server's primitives (tools/resources/prompts/tasks/UI) each have security implications the gateway must handle. **No piece works in isolation** — they form a **system** where each layer depends on and reinforces the others.
- **Architectural judgment is choosing what goes where:** *why* the security is at the gateway (not the client), *why* observability wraps the loop (not the tools), *why* hash-pinning is enforced centrally — these are **system-design decisions** about **where each concern belongs**, which is exactly what distinguishes a robust production system from a pile of components. The capstone's stated goal — **'defend every architectural choice'** — is the test of this skill: can you explain *why* each layer is where it is and *what* would break otherwise?
- **Knowing individual technologies isn't enough** — production engineering is **assembling them correctly**, understanding the **interactions, the right placement of each concern (security at the chokepoint, observability at the orchestration layer, portability via standards), and the failure modes each layer prevents**. That **system-composition skill** — not any single API — is what the phase builds toward. The capstone proves you can take 22 lessons of pieces and **wire them into one defensible, production-shaped tool ecosystem.**

exercises.title: Production-readiness, the security/observability/portability throughlines, and the agent-ecosystem foundation
exercises.prompt: Assess the capstone system for production-readiness: trace the security throughline (untrusted servers → defense-in-depth), the observability throughline (one OTel trace), and the portability throughline (open standards). Explain how this tool ecosystem is the foundation for agent engineering (Phase 14) and autonomous systems (Phase 15), and the remaining gaps to a fully autonomous, safe production agent.
exercises.hint: Security throughline: servers untrusted → auth (OAuth) + poisoning defense (hash-pin/scan) + least-privilege (roots/scopes/RBAC) + human-in-loop (elicitation) + gateway enforcement. Observability: one OTel trace across all hops. Portability: MCP/A2A/AGENTS.md/SKILL.md open standards. Foundation for Phase 14/15 agents. Gaps: prompt injection unsolved, long-horizon reliability, autonomy safety.
exercises.solution:
**The three throughlines (assessing production-readiness):**

**Security throughline (untrusted servers → defense-in-depth):** the entire MCP security model rests on **'servers are untrusted; the host/gateway is the boundary'** (Lessons 8, 15-18), and the capstone implements **defense-in-depth**:
- **Authorization (OAuth 2.1, Lessons 16, 18):** audience-pinned, scoped, least-privilege tokens — *who can access what*.
- **Poisoning defense (Lesson 15):** scan descriptions + **hash-pinning** (rug-pull detection) in CI and at the gateway — *secure the context surface*.
- **Least-privilege (roots Lesson 12, scopes Lesson 16, RBAC Lesson 17):** confine what each server/user can do — *contain the blast radius*.
- **Human-in-the-loop (elicitation, Lesson 12):** confirm irreversible actions — *gate dangerous operations*.
- **Gateway enforcement (Lesson 17):** all of the above enforced **centrally, unbypassably, with audit**.
- **Treat all server content as untrusted** (tool descriptions/results/resources/prompts/sampling/UI) — the consistent discipline.
This is **layered defense** because **no single layer suffices** (prompt injection is unsolved, >85% attack success vs single defenses, Lesson 15) — you **assume injection succeeds and contain the damage** (least-privilege + human-in-loop + audit). *Production-ready security = defense-in-depth, not a single control.*

**Observability throughline (one OTel trace):** **one OpenTelemetry GenAI trace spans the whole system** (Lesson 20) — agent loop → LLM calls → gateway → MCP servers → A2A sub-agents — with **standard attributes (model, tokens, cost, latency, status)**, content **redacted by default** (privacy). This makes the distributed agent **debuggable, cost-trackable, latency-analyzable, and regression-detectable** — *you can see what the agent actually did*. *Production-ready operations = standardized end-to-end observability.*

**Portability throughline (open standards):** every layer uses an **open standard** — **MCP** (tools), **A2A** (agents), **OAuth 2.1/OTel** (auth/observability), **AGENTS.md/SKILL.md** (context/know-how) — so the system is **portable, interoperable, and not locked to one vendor/framework** (collapses N×M to N+M). *Production-ready architecture = built on open standards for portability and interoperability.*
Together the three throughlines (**secure, observable, portable**) are the marks of a production-ready system — and the capstone embodies all three.

**Foundation for agent engineering (Phase 14) and autonomous systems (Phase 15):** this tool ecosystem is the **substrate agents are built on**:
- **Phase 14 (Agent Engineering)** builds **reliable, long-horizon agents** — and they need **exactly this foundation**: standard tools (MCP), the four-step loop (Lesson 1), structured I/O (Lesson 4), parallel/async execution (Lessons 3, 13), security (Lessons 15-18), observability (Lesson 20), and portable packaging (Lesson 22). Phase 14's agent-reliability work (memory, planning, error recovery) **assumes this tool/protocol substrate exists**.
- **Phase 15 (Autonomous Systems)** builds **autonomous agents** that act with less human oversight — which makes the **security/containment** layers (least-privilege, human-in-loop, audit) and **observability** even more critical (an autonomous agent's mistakes are higher-stakes). The capstone's defense-in-depth and tracing are **prerequisites for safe autonomy**.
This phase provides the **'how agents access tools and collaborate, safely and observably'** foundation that Phases 14-15's **'how agents reason, plan, remember, and act autonomously'** are built on. Tools/protocols are the **hands and nervous system**; agent engineering is the **brain and reliability**.

**Remaining gaps to a fully autonomous, safe production agent:**
- **Prompt injection is unsolved (Lesson 15, Phase 11.12):** the capstone *contains* it (defense-in-depth) but can't *prevent* it — autonomous agents acting on untrusted content remain fundamentally vulnerable, so **human-in-the-loop and least-privilege stay essential** for high-stakes actions.
- **Long-horizon reliability (Phase 14):** the tool substrate is solid, but **agents still struggle with long, multi-step tasks** (error compounding, recovery, memory — Phase 12.25's ~30% on hard agent benchmarks). The reasoning/reliability layer isn't solved.
- **Autonomy safety (Phase 15):** the more autonomous the agent, the more the **safety, alignment, and oversight** questions (Phase 18) bite — when do you let an agent act without confirmation? How do you bound a fully autonomous agent? These are open.
- **Cost/latency at scale, evaluation of agentic behavior, and emerging-standard drift** (several Phase 13 features were experimental through H1 2026).
**The synthesis:** the capstone proves you can **compose the entire tools-and-protocols stack into one secure (defense-in-depth), observable (OTel), portable (open standards) production-shaped system** — the **foundation** on which reliable agents (Phase 14) and safe autonomous systems (Phase 15) are built. It's **production-ready as a tool/protocol substrate**, but the **gaps to fully autonomous, safe agents** (unsolved injection, long-horizon reliability, autonomy safety) are **exactly what the next phases address**. Phase 13 gives agents **safe, observable, portable access to tools and each other**; Phases 14-15 give them **the reliability and bounded autonomy to use that access well** — and the capstone is the bridge, demonstrating the complete, defensible tool ecosystem that everything after stands on.
